The generic source-description note linking the text to the former Seton and Pennant manuscript is misaligned carrier metadata and does not identify this work's witness.

bibliographic.citation: A Fifteenth-Century Courtesy Book, ed. R. W. Chambers, and Two Fifteenth-Century Franciscan Rules, ed. Walter W. Seton, Early English Text Society, Original Series 148 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., and Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1914).
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., and Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 148
bibliographic.title: A Fifteenth-Century Courtesy Book and Two Fifteenth-Century Franciscan Rules

entities.description: R. W. Chambers's opening component of a composite EETS volume. His courtesy-book component comprises a title page, introduction (pp. 3-8), the complete three-section text (pp. 11-17), notes (pp. 18-22), and a glossary beginning at p. 125. Walter W. Seton edited the two following Franciscan components, not this tract.
entities.label: Chambers 1914 edition of A Generall Rule
notes: The XML title Fifteenth-century courtesy book. Selections reflects electronic cataloging and does not make Chambers's complete tract an edited selection.
The former Seton and Pennant witness described in the carrier belongs to a Franciscan component edited by Seton, not to Chambers's Add MS 37969 tract.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1914, before 1 January 1931; the U.S. Copyright Office's April 2026 duration guidance states that U.S.-published works from before that date are public domain, and U-M currently applies No Copyright-United States to CME materials.
rights.component: The medieval text and Chambers's 1914 transcription, introduction, notes, and glossary

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century paper manuscript containing the household-service tract at fols. 2r-8r. Separate material comprises wood memoranda partly concerning Talaton on fol. 1, English and Latin medical recipes on fol. 8v, and a vellum former-binding fragment with fifteenth-century accounts.

holding.notes: The current catalog describes the work as a single Generall Rule at fols. 2r-8r and distinguishes adjacent memoranda and recipes.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 37969

physicalDescription.extent: i + 8 folios; the cataloged work occupies fols. 2r-8r
notes: The sole work colophon is Explicit a good techyng at fol. 8r.
The current catalog records English as the manuscript language and Latin script; English and Latin recipes on fol. 8v are outside the work.
Talaton appears in separate wood memoranda and is not recorded as the manuscript's production place.
physicalDescription.support: Paper, except for a vellum former-binding fragment

relations.subjectLabel: General Corpus source XML for RuleServLd

reviewNotes: The cataloged work is the one Middle English tract at Add MS 37969 fols. 2r-8r, with three integral rubricated sections and one final explicit; it is not three independently attested works and not a Chambers-created selection.
The publication-set filename and repository work ID are RuleServLd, while the XML IDNO, DIV1 NODE values, and U-M handle use RuleServeLd. The discrepancy is preserved rather than normalized away.

The XML source-description note Derived from olim Seton, olim Pennant (whereabouts unknown) is inherited from the Franciscan portion of the composite 1914 carrier. Chambers identifies Add MS 37969 as the courtesy tract's witness, so no Pennant or Seton provenance edge is attached to this work.

The XML declares Latin only, but direct inspection finds Middle English throughout the cataloged body except the one-word rubric marker Nota. Latin medical recipes on fol. 8v are outside the work; Latin is retained in this graph only because the broader witness and composite carrier contain it.
The repository XML has 25 P elements in total, but only 20 are in BODY; the other 5 occur in HEADER. The three BODY divisions contain 18, 1, and 1 paragraphs.
Talaton, Devon, is associated with separate flyleaf wood memoranda, not with a demonstrated composition or copying place for the tract.
The absence finding for a public complete manuscript facsimile is dated 2026-07-15 and does not claim that the manuscript has never been photographed or digitized.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a local CME-submodule representation of University of Michigan item RuleServeLd, produced in 1999 by scanning and ScanWorx OCR from R. W. Chambers's contribution to EETS Original Series 148 (1914). Chambers transcribed the complete three-section household-service tract from British Library Add MS 37969, fols. 2r-8r. U-M retains the component title page and printed text at pp. 11-17 but omits Chambers's introduction, notes, and glossary; print italics marking abbreviation expansions become silent. A complete scan of the 1914 composite book is public through Internet Archive. No verified public complete manuscript facsimile was found in the exact current British Library catalog or its 10 October 2025 digitised-manuscript list; the manuscript remains consultable onsite and photography can be ordered subject to service review and terms.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composition undated; first attested at the end of the fifteenth century
catalogSummary.region: England; exact composition and copying place unknown
catalogSummary.title: A Generall Rule to Teche Euery Man That Is Willynge for to Lerne, to Serve a Lorde or Mayster in Euery Thyng to His Plesure

catalogingSubject.label: A Generall Rule to Teche Euery Man That Is Willynge for to Lerne, to Serve a Lorde or Mayster in Euery Thyng to His Plesure
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The manuscript-defined Middle English household-service tract in British Library Add MS 37969, fols. 2r-8r. Its three consecutively transmitted rubricated components cover service to a lord, the order of fish dishes, and a gentleman carver's conduct; the single closing explicit and the British Library's unitary catalog description support treating them as integral sections of one tract. The record does not describe the manuscript's flyleaf memoranda, medical recipes on fol. 8v, the whole 1914 composite EETS volume, its Franciscan works, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Household-service tract
notes: The parent carries the one unique work count; child sections carry zero additional items to avoid double counting.
contentParts.summary: The complete tract on household service, fish-course order, and the conduct of a gentleman carver.

contentParts.label: Rule for serving a lord or master
notes: The encoding represents this section with 18 body paragraphs.
contentParts.summary: Defines the duties, hierarchy, conduct, meal service, provisioning, lodging, accounting, and disciplinary authority of great-household officers and servants.

contentParts.label: Rule for serving fish
contentParts.summary: Orders preserved, fried, sea, river, pond, roasted, shell, baked, and delicate fish dishes across successive courses.

contentParts.label: Rule for a gentleman carver
notes: The manuscript-defined tract ends after this section with Explicit a good techyng.
contentParts.summary: Instructs a gentleman carver in towel placement, carrying and presenting food, kneeling or standing according to the lord's posture, and formal obedience.

dateStatements.display: No later than the end of the fifteenth century; otherwise undated
notes: The manuscript date supplies a terminus ante quem for the text represented by this copy, not an exact composition date.

dateStatements.display: British Library Add MS 37969, end of the fifteenth century

extent.description: One manuscript-defined prose tract with three integral sections. The repository XML BODY contains 3 DIV1 elements and 20 P elements (18 + 1 + 1), not 25 body paragraphs; the whole XML has 25 P because 5 occur in the header. The body also preserves 7 print-page breaks, 12 manuscript-leaf milestones, one Latin Nota marker, one deletion, and no verse-line elements. These are representation metrics, not counts of separate works.

formStatements.description: Instructional prose in three consecutively transmitted sections; the repository body contains no verse-line elements.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The lone Latin rubric marker Nota is too slight to justify a Latin work-level language facet. Latin medical recipes on fol. 8v are outside the cataloged work.
The publication-set filename and work ID are RuleServLd, while the XML IDNO and all DIV1 NODE values are RuleServeLd; both forms are preserved with their layer-specific scopes.

The XML source-description note Derived from olim Seton, olim Pennant (whereabouts unknown) was inherited from the Franciscan portion of the composite 1914 carrier and is not provenance evidence for British Library Add MS 37969.

The XML PROFILEDESC declares only Latin, but direct text inspection shows Middle English throughout apart from the single rubric marker Nota; the manifest therefore records only Middle English at work level.
Rights, provider terms, reproduction access, and the current absence of a verified public complete manuscript facsimile are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult a current census and scholarship on Middle English household and courtesy texts.
Retain the three parts as integral sections of the attested Add MS 37969 tract unless independent witnesses or source relations are identified.
openQuestions.question: Did the fish-service and carver sections circulate independently before their transmission with the principal household-service rule?

next_steps: Consult current linguistic, paleographical, and household-literature scholarship.
Do not convert the manuscript date or Talaton flyleaf memoranda into a precise composition date or place.
openQuestions.question: When, where, and by whom was the household-service tract composed or compiled?

notes: The Middle English language and English household context support a broad English cultural origin. Talaton, Devon, appears only in separate flyleaf wood memoranda and is not evidence for composition or copying place.
No author is named. The reviewed evidence does not establish whether one person composed or assembled all three integral sections.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous scribe of British Library Add MS 37969
responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous scribe of British Library Add MS 37969
notes: Witness-level scribal responsibility is not authorship.

scope.description: Copying of the household-service tract at fols. 2r-8r
Transcription from British Museum Additional MS 37969, now British Library Add MS 37969
Introduction, notes, and glossary in the 1914 edition; omitted from the U-M encoding

subjects.label: Household food service and course organization

summaries.value: An anonymous late-medieval Middle English tract governing service in a great household. Its principal section assigns duties and authority to the marshal, usher, steward, sewer, carver, almoner, pantry and buttery staff, and other servants before, during, and after meals; two shorter concluding sections order the service of fish dishes and instruct a gentleman carver in handling a towel, food, posture, and deference.

The British Library catalogs the text at Add MS 37969 fols. 2r-8r as one Generall Rule and includes the fish rule within that description. Chambers likewise calls the transmitted sequence one tract and prints its three rubricated sections continuously, followed by the sole explicit. The sections are therefore represented as integral components rather than three independently attested works or an editorial selection.

titles.value: A Generall Rule to Teche Euery Man That Is Willynge for to Lerne, to Serve a Lorde or Mayster in Euery Thyng to His Plesure

notes: Modern short title used for Chambers's component of the 1914 composite volume.

work.summary: An anonymous late-medieval Middle English tract governing service in a great household. Its principal section assigns duties and authority to the marshal, usher, steward, sewer, carver, almoner, pantry and buttery staff, and other servants before, during, and after meals; two shorter concluding sections order the service of fish dishes and instruct a gentleman carver in handling a towel, food, posture, and deference.
